questions: At CyanGate, you led the development of the **Atlassian Jira Intelligence System**, which involved coordinating across multiple technologies (Copilot Studio, Power Automate, Azure AI Search, Python, PostgreSQL) and stakeholders—from technical implementation to client requirements. This kind of project often requires navigating ambiguous priorities, especially when balancing *technical feasibility* with *client expectations*. Can you walk me through a specific moment where you had to push back on a client’s request or realign their expectations because their ask wasn’t technically sound or scalable? How did you communicate this, and what was the outcome for both the project timeline *and* the client relationship?

You’ve achieved remarkable success in competitive AI challenges—gold in TEKNOFEST’s NLP competition, bronze in the E-Commerce AI challenge, and second place in the GIV Entrepreneurship Competition—all while working full-time at CyanGate. These competitions often demand intense focus under tight deadlines, similar to the high-pressure, fast-iterative environment at Zephlen. Tell me about a time during one of these competitions (or a high-stakes project at work) where you hit a wall—whether it was burnout, a technical dead-end, or team conflict. How did you recognize the issue, adjust your approach, and what did you learn about your own limits or work style from that experience?

Zephlen’s culture emphasizes *‘radical candor and low ego’*—we debate ideas vigorously, but once a decision is made, we commit fully. In your **JSON-to-JSON AI Agentic System** project at CyanGate, you integrated multiple LLM providers (OpenAI, Copilot, Gemini, Anthropic) and had to make architecture decisions that likely involved trade-offs (e.g., cost vs. accuracy, latency vs. flexibility). Describe a situation where you *strongly disagreed* with a teammate, manager, or client about a technical or design choice. How did you advocate for your position, and when/why did you ultimately concede or double down? How do you separate *personal investment in an idea* from *what’s best for the project*?
